item.title: Contact
item.dc:creator: yingxzclimate
item.content:encoded:
Feel free to reach out to me using the contact information on the left or the contact form on the right. Please provide 3-5 business days for a response.

Ways to reach me

Department of Climate and Space Sciences and Engineering University of Michigan
Space Research Building
2455 Hayward St.
Ann Arbor, Michigan 48109-2143
USA
yingxz(@)umich.edu
(734) 881-5404

Send me a Message

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value:
[contact-field required="1" type="name" label="Name"/]
[contact-field label="Email" required="1" type="email"/]
[contact-field type="url" label="Website"/]
[contact-field required="1" type="textarea" label="Message"/]

item.title: About
item.dc:creator: yingxzclimate
item.content:encoded:
Yingxiao Zhang – Ph.D. candidate in University of Michigan

I'm interested in Earth’s ecosystem responses to climatic changes and the associate feedbacks. During my doctoral training, my ultimate goal is to develop into a proficient scholar, who can conduct research at the frontier of atmospheric science and provide valuable information to decision makers that co-operate with academia. 

    • Nanjing University

      • B.S. in Atmospheric Sciences
        • 2015-2019

    • University of California, Los Angeles

      • Summer internship
        • Supervisor: Jasper Kok
        • 2018

    • University of Michigan

      • PhD candidate
        • Advisor: Allison Steiner
        • 2019-2024 (expected)

item.title: Introduce Yourself (Example Post)
item.dc:creator: yingxzclimate
item.content:encoded:
This is an example post, originally published as part of Blogging University. Enroll in one of our ten programs, and start your blog right.

You’re going to publish a post today. Don’t worry about how your blog looks. Don’t worry if you haven’t given it a name yet, or you’re feeling overwhelmed. Just click the “New Post” button, and tell us why you’re here.

Why do this?

  • Because it gives new readers context. What are you about? Why should they read your blog?
  • Because it will help you focus your own ideas about your blog and what you’d like to do with it.

The post can be short or long, a personal intro to your life or a bloggy mission statement, a manifesto for the future or a simple outline of your the types of things you hope to publish.

To help you get started, here are a few questions:

  • Why are you blogging publicly, rather than keeping a personal journal?
  • What topics do you think you’ll write about?
  • Who would you love to connect with via your blog?
  • If you blog successfully throughout the next year, what would you hope to have accomplished?

You’re not locked into any of this; one of the wonderful things about blogs is how they constantly evolve as we learn, grow, and interact with one another — but it’s good to know where and why you started, and articulating your goals may just give you a few other post ideas.

Can’t think how to get started? Just write the first thing that pops into your head. Anne Lamott, author of a book on writing we love, says that you need to give yourself permission to write a “crappy first draft”. Anne makes a great point — just start writing, and worry about editing it later.

When you’re ready to publish, give your post three to five tags that describe your blog’s focus — writing, photography, fiction, parenting, food, cars, movies, sports, whatever. These tags will help others who care about your topics find you in the Reader. Make sure one of the tags is “zerotohero,” so other new bloggers can find you, too.

item.title: Introduce Yourself (Example Post)
item.dc:creator: yingxzclimate
item.content:encoded:
This is an example post, originally published as part of Blogging University. Enroll in one of our ten programs, and start your blog right.

You’re going to publish a post today. Don’t worry about how your blog looks. Don’t worry if you haven’t given it a name yet, or you’re feeling overwhelmed. Just click the “New Post” button, and tell us why you’re here.

Why do this?

  • Because it gives new readers context. What are you about? Why should they read your blog?
  • Because it will help you focus your own ideas about your blog and what you’d like to do with it.

The post can be short or long, a personal intro to your life or a bloggy mission statement, a manifesto for the future or a simple outline of your the types of things you hope to publish.

To help you get started, here are a few questions:

  • Why are you blogging publicly, rather than keeping a personal journal?
  • What topics do you think you’ll write about?
  • Who would you love to connect with via your blog?
  • If you blog successfully throughout the next year, what would you hope to have accomplished?

You’re not locked into any of this; one of the wonderful things about blogs is how they constantly evolve as we learn, grow, and interact with one another — but it’s good to know where and why you started, and articulating your goals may just give you a few other post ideas.

Can’t think how to get started? Just write the first thing that pops into your head. Anne Lamott, author of a book on writing we love, says that you need to give yourself permission to write a “crappy first draft”. Anne makes a great point — just start writing, and worry about editing it later.

When you’re ready to publish, give your post three to five tags that describe your blog’s focus — writing, photography, fiction, parenting, food, cars, movies, sports, whatever. These tags will help others who care about your topics find you in the Reader. Make sure one of the tags is “zerotohero,” so other new bloggers can find you, too.

item.title: Introduce Yourself (Example Post)
item.dc:creator: yingxzclimate
item.content:encoded:
This is an example post, originally published as part of Blogging University. Enroll in one of our ten programs, and start your blog right.

You’re going to publish a post today. Don’t worry about how your blog looks. Don’t worry if you haven’t given it a name yet, or you’re feeling overwhelmed. Just click the “New Post” button, and tell us why you’re here.

Why do this?

  • Because it gives new readers context. What are you about? Why should they read your blog?
  • Because it will help you focus your own ideas about your blog and what you’d like to do with it.

The post can be short or long, a personal intro to your life or a bloggy mission statement, a manifesto for the future or a simple outline of your the types of things you hope to publish.

To help you get started, here are a few questions:

  • Why are you blogging publicly, rather than keeping a personal journal?
  • What topics do you think you’ll write about?
  • Who would you love to connect with via your blog?
  • If you blog successfully throughout the next year, what would you hope to have accomplished?

You’re not locked into any of this; one of the wonderful things about blogs is how they constantly evolve as we learn, grow, and interact with one another — but it’s good to know where and why you started, and articulating your goals may just give you a few other post ideas.

Can’t think how to get started? Just write the first thing that pops into your head. Anne Lamott, author of a book on writing we love, says that you need to give yourself permission to write a “crappy first draft”. Anne makes a great point — just start writing, and worry about editing it later.

When you’re ready to publish, give your post three to five tags that describe your blog’s focus — writing, photography, fiction, parenting, food, cars, movies, sports, whatever. These tags will help others who care about your topics find you in the Reader. Make sure one of the tags is “zerotohero,” so other new bloggers can find you, too.

item.title: CV
item.dc:creator: yingxzclimate
item.content:encoded:
Download my CV

PUBLICATION

  • Zhang, Y. and Steiner, A. “Projected pollen emissions under future climate warming”, manuscript in preparation for Nature Climate Change

PRESENTATIONS AND CONFERENCES

  • Zhang, Y. and Steiner, A. “A092-0005: Modeling pollen emission variations over the continental U.S. with climate change”, presented at American Geophysical Union (AGU), Online, December, 2020 
  • Zhang, Y., Lin, C., Lin, Y., Xie Y. “Investigation on the effect of daytime precipitation induced cold pool on nighttime precipitation”, presented at the Peking University Atmospheric Science Forum, Beijing, April, 2018

Other:

  • Member, American Geophysical Union, 2020-present

PROFESSIONAL EXPERIENCE

item.title: Teaching
item.dc:creator: yingxzclimate
item.content:encoded:
• Capstone Project Mentor in Girls Who Code Club (Winter 2021)

Assist local high school girls to work on a research project using data analysis and visualization tools (e.g., Python).

  • Teaching assistant in Jianye Road school, Nanjing, China (2017-2018)

Co-instructed weekly English classes for 1st – 5th grade students with English native speakers.

item.title: Research
item.dc:creator: yingxzclimate
item.content:encoded:
Projected future pollen emission change

Anemophilous (or wind-driven) pollen plays an important role in plant fertilization and gene dispersal, can affect climate by interacting with clouds and radiation, and is the major cause of allergic diseases such as allergic rhinitis (also known as hay fever) and asthma. Atmospheric conditions affect the release of anemophilous (wind-driven) pollen, and the timing and magnitude of pollen released will be altered by climate change. 

In our study, we project to evaluate pollen emission change at the end of century over the United States. We simulate a shift in the pollen season, an increase in the pollen season duration, and in some locations, an increase in the magnitude of pollen emissions. Interestingly, we find that in some locations such as the Northeastern US, spring seasonal shifts increase the overlap of different vegetation taxa, increasing maximum daily pollen emission up to 20%. Together, we suggest that future allergy conditions will become worse due to these changes in pollen emission.

item.title: Home
item.dc:creator: yingxzclimate
item.content:encoded:
Hello, I'm Yingxiao,

I am a Climate Science PhD candidate at University of Michigan. My research interest is exploring Earth’s ecosystem responses to climatic changes and the associate feedbacks.

Learn more

Get new content delivered directly to your inbox.
